title: Advanced Context Engineering Capstone
concept: Designing a full context pipeline for a multi-step agent under a strict token budget; 'Next up: Embeddings Deep Dive.'.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why give each sub-agent or sub-task a fresh, scoped context instead of one giant shared window?

options.text: So irrelevant details from other sub-tasks don't clutter up and confuse the current task
So the agent can forget the user's original goal completely
So every sub-task uses the maximum possible tokens available

exercises.explain: Context isolation keeps each sub-task clean and focused — like giving each worker their own clipboard instead of one giant messy shared notebook. This keeps token usage efficient and reduces confusion.

cards.title: The Final Challenge
cards.body: Your agent has a big job to do — search, analyze, write — but only a tiny token budget to work with. Can you design the context pipeline that makes it all fit without breaking?

cards.title: What Is a Context Pipeline?
cards.body: A context pipeline is the step-by-step system that decides what goes into an agent's prompt at each turn: what to retrieve, what to summarize, what to isolate, and what to trim. Under a strict token budget, every piece must earn its spot.

cards.title: Packing a carry-on, not a moving truck
cards.body: A token budget is like a carry-on suitcase for a trip: you can't bring your whole closet. You pick the outfits you'll actually wear (relevant memory, tools, retrieved facts) and leave the rest in storage, ready to fetch if needed.

cards.title: A Research Agent's Turn
cards.body: Step 1: fresh scoped context for 'search web'. Step 2: summarize findings into 3 bullet points instead of raw text. Step 3: pass only those bullets plus the original question to 'write report'. Step 4: reserve 500 tokens for the final answer so it never gets cut off.

cards.title: Myth: More context always means smarter agents
cards.body: False! Stuffing every past message and every tool doc into the prompt often confuses the model, wastes budget, and pushes out the truly important info. Precise, well-chosen context beats maximal context almost every time.

cards.title: Bloated vs. Budgeted Pipeline
cards.body: Compare an agent that hoards everything versus one that carefully curates its context under a budget.

left.text: Full chat history + full tool docs + every retrieved doc, every single turn — budget blown, key info buried

right.text: Scoped sub-task context + top relevant chunks + short summaries + reserved space for the answer — lean and effective

exercises.prompt: In a multi-step agent pipeline under a strict token budget, why should you summarize completed steps before moving to the next step?

options.text: Summaries make the agent forget everything on purpose
Summaries compress what happened into fewer tokens, freeing budget for new work while keeping key facts
Summaries are required by law for AI systems
Summaries make the model respond slower

exercises.explain: Summarizing turns a long, token-heavy history into a short recap that still carries the important facts forward, which is essential when every token counts.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A good context pipeline should always try to include the maximum possible amount of information in every prompt, regardless of the token budget.
exercises.explain: False — under a strict budget, cramming in maximum information often crowds out the most important details and confuses the model. The goal is relevant, well-curated context, not maximal context.

exercises.sentence: Under a strict token budget, a good context pipeline retrieves only relevant chunks instead of dumping the entire history.
exercises.explain: Retrieving only relevant chunks keeps the prompt focused and leaves room in the budget for other essentials like instructions and the final answer.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three context pipeline descriptions was most likely written by an AI trying to sound impressive but actually being wasteful and vague?

options.text: 'Our pipeline retrieves the top 3 relevant chunks, summarizes prior steps into short bullets, and reserves 20% of the budget for the final answer.'
'Our pipeline leverages comprehensive holistic context synthesis to maximize situational awareness across all conceivable data dimensions for optimal synergy.'
'Our pipeline gives each sub-task a fresh scoped context and trims old messages once they're summarized.'

exercises.explain: The second option is full of vague buzzwords ('holistic', 'synergy', 'all conceivable data dimensions') with no concrete plan for what's included, excluded, or budgeted — a red flag for AI-generated fluff rather than a real engineering design.

exercises.prompt: You're designing context for a multi-step research agent with a strict 8,000-token budget per call. Which pipeline design best balances capability and budget?

options.text: Dump the entire conversation history and every tool's full documentation into every single call
Give every sub-task a fresh, scoped context; retrieve only relevant chunks; summarize old steps; and reserve a fixed slice of tokens for the final answer
Only give the agent the very first user message and nothing else, to save tokens
Let the agent decide randomly each turn whether to include past steps or not

exercises.explain: A well-engineered pipeline treats the token budget like a strict packing list: isolate sub-tasks so junk doesn't pile up, retrieve only what's relevant instead of everything, compress old steps into summaries, and always protect room for the output. This combines everything from the unit — retrieval, isolation, summarization, and budgeting — into one working system. Next up: Embeddings Deep Dive.
